notes.note: The fanum is the memorial shrine Cicero is determined to build for his dead daughter Tullia, conceived not as an ordinary tomb but as a cult-site for her apotheosis (deification). The project dominates the Astura letters of 45 BC and drives his hunt for suburban gardens to house it.

notes.anchor: that I may attain, so far as possible, an apotheosis
notes.note: Apotheosis (Greek apotheosis) is the elevation of a mortal to divine status; Cicero wants Tullia's shrine to consecrate her as a goddess rather than merely commemorate her. The aim distinguishes a fanum from a sepulcrum (tomb) and shapes the whole gardens scheme.

notes.anchor: not so much to avoid the law's penalty
notes.note: Roman law (ultimately the Twelve Tables) restricted burial within the city and penalised converting a private tomb into a cult-site; Cicero wants the law's text from Atticus so he can avoid making the shrine look like a tomb. His worry is both legal and religious propriety.

notes.anchor: because he would not be at the Cumanum
notes.note: The Cumanum was Cicero's villa at Cumae on the Bay of Naples, a favourite writing-retreat; he is annoyed that Brutus declined to meet him there. The exchange shows Brutus among the friends consoling Cicero in his grief.

Cluatius was the architect Cicero engaged to design the fanum for Tullia; Cicero wants Atticus to keep him at work even before a site is settled. He is named only in this gardens-and-shrine correspondence of 45 BC.
